symbols: mandorla (vesica piscis) frame — the intersection of heaven and earth, the divine glory surrounding Christ
Christ standing with children — fulfillment of Jesus's command to let the little children come to Him
white marble — purity and the eternal, unchanging nature of Christ
flowing robe — Christ's priestly and kingly dignity
gold border — divine majesty and heavenly light
description: A white marble bas-relief set within a mandorla-shaped (pointed oval) frame with a gold inner border, mounted against a deep crimson wall. The relief depicts a standing figure of Christ draped in a flowing robe, with long hair parted in the center. Two small children accompany him -- one stands at his right side, reaching up to hold his hand, while another sits at his left with a basket. The neoclassical style renders the figures in smooth, idealized forms with restrained emotion.
theological_description: This relief presents Christ as the Salvator Mundi -- Savior of the World -- in a composition that specifically emphasizes His tenderness toward children. The image draws directly from the Synoptic Gospel accounts where Jesus rebuked the disciples for turning children away, declaring, 'Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these' (Matthew 19:14). The mandorla frame, a shape used since early Christian art to denote divine glory and the intersection of the heavenly and earthly realms, elevates the scene from a simple genre depiction to a theological statement: Christ's welcoming of children is a revelation of the nature of God's kingdom itself. The neoclassical idiom, with its restrained dignity, presents Christ not as a suffering redeemer but as a gentle shepherd and teacher. The basket beside the seated child may allude to divine provision and the bread of life. Placed in a church setting (suggested by the liturgical crimson wall), the relief would serve as a visual catechesis on baptism and the place of children in the covenant community.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus's command to let the children come to Him and His declaration that the kingdom belongs to such as these -- the direct subject of the relief.
Mark's account of Jesus blessing the children, taking them in His arms -- the Gospel event depicted.
Unless you become like little children you will not enter the kingdom -- the theological teaching embodied in the composition.
Out of the mouth of babes and infants God has established praise -- the dignity of children in God's economy.
